steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses joined without a coordinating conjunction: (1) "A team led by researcher Mark E. Ritchie collected arthropods with [sweep nets, for instance]" and (2) "another research team, led by Alison G. Boyer, gathered them with bug vacuums." Two independent clauses joined without a coordinating conjunction require a semicolon between them. Within the first clause, "for instance" is a parenthetical adverb that must be set off by commas on both sides.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'sweep nets; for instance,': places the semicolon before "for instance," which splits the first clause in two and leaves the second clause beginning with a lowercase fragment. ✗
  • Choice B — 'sweep nets for instance': omits all punctuation around the parenthetical "for instance" and between the two independent clauses — both errors at once. ✗
  • Choice C — 'sweep nets, for instance,': correctly punctuates the parenthetical but uses only a comma to join the two independent clauses, creating a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — 'sweep nets, for instance;': commas around the parenthetical "for instance" and a semicolon after the first independent clause to join the second. Both rules satisfied. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
